items.title: Embeddings stored as Python lists
items.scenario: Loading 100K embeddings takes 8 GB; numpy would take 300 MB.
items.explanation: tracemalloc points at `embeddings.append(list(vec))`. Switch to `np.zeros((n, 768), dtype='float32')`; memory drops 25×, no algorithm change.

items.title: lru_cache leaks the world
items.scenario: A long-running service slowly climbs from 1 GB to 12 GB then OOMs.
items.explanation: `memray --leaks` shows growth concentrated in a `@lru_cache(maxsize=None)` keyed by user query strings — every unique query lives forever. Set `maxsize=10_000` and add a TTL eviction; leak stops.

items.title: Long-running notebook RAM creep
items.scenario: Jupyter notebook reaches 30 GB after a few hours of iteration.
items.explanation: Earlier cells loaded huge dataframes you've since stopped using. `del big_df; import gc; gc.collect()` or restart the kernel. Use `%memit` to check before/after.

items.title: Picking the right batch size
items.scenario: Embedding throughput is good but peak RAM spikes to 16 GB on batch=512.
items.explanation: Profile batch=64, 256, 512 with `memray` flamegraph. Pick the smallest batch whose peak fits the prod machine; trade some throughput for stability.

items.title: mmap-backed FAISS index
items.scenario: 10 GB index keeps the whole vector store in RAM even when queries touch a tiny subset.
items.explanation: Load the index in mmap mode (`faiss.read_index(path, faiss.IO_FLAG_MMAP)`). RSS drops to a few hundred MB; the kernel pages in what's needed. tracemalloc shows the Python-side allocations stay constant.
